quick_facts.label: Core proposition
quick_facts.value: A planning-consultancy venture can sell spatial-planning, GIS/spatial-analysis, infrastructure, environmental-planning and policy-advisory work to public, private and development-sector clients; these are capabilities reflected in India’s B.Plan model curriculum and planning-school curricula.

quick_facts.label: Education base
quick_facts.value: B.Plan is a relevant starting qualification: the national model curriculum includes urban and regional planning, planning techniques, spatial-data infrastructure, environmental planning, planning legislation, project appraisal and management, and dissertation work.

quick_facts.label: EIA boundary
quick_facts.value: For environmental-clearance EIA/EMP submissions, the consultancy must meet the applicable QCI–NABET accreditation requirement; QCI states that MoEFCC mandated use of QCI–NABET-accredited consultants for preparing and presenting EIA-EMPs to regulatory authorities.

quick_facts.label: Company incorporation
quick_facts.value: Where the founders choose a company structure, MCA’s SPICe+ and AGILE-PRO-S workflow provides integrated services including name reservation, incorporation, PAN, TAN, DIN, GST registration if applied for, bank account, and specified labour/state registrations.

quick_facts.label: MSME option
quick_facts.value: An eligible consultancy may obtain Udyam registration as a service enterprise. The official portal describes registration as free, paperless, online and based on self-declaration; it also states that a permanent registration number is issued and renewal is not required.

quick_facts.label: GST baseline
quick_facts.value: The general statutory registration threshold under CGST Act section 22 is aggregate turnover exceeding ₹20 lakh in a financial year in non-special-category States/UTs; exceptions and special-category-State rules can apply, so the firm should obtain current tax advice before contracting or invoicing.

sections.heading: From B.Plan to an independent planning consultancy
paragraphs: A B.Plan graduate can build toward entrepreneurship by first converting academic outputs into a credible practice portfolio: studio plans, surveys, GIS maps, land-use or infrastructure analyses, environmental-planning work, policy notes, and a dissertation. This is aligned with the B.Plan model curriculum, which covers planning techniques, quantitative and qualitative methods, spatial-data infrastructure, planning communication, urban and regional infrastructure, ecology and resource planning, environmental planning, development finance, planning legislation, project appraisal and management, and dissertation writing.

A practical route is to gain project-delivery experience with a planning consultancy, urban local body, development authority, research organisation, NGO, or multidisciplinary design/environment firm; then launch with a focused service line and expand through associates. SPA Bhopal describes B.Plan preparation as encompassing urban and regional development, infrastructure, environmental planning and policy analysis, and identifies drafting, spatial analysis, GIS mapping and urban-development work as graduate activities in consultancies, government and NGOs.

Further study is optional rather than a prerequisite for starting a firm. It can nevertheless support technical positioning in urban and regional, environmental, transport, or related planning specialisms, which are offered as distinct planning programmes at Schools of Planning and Architecture.
bullets: Build demonstrable samples with clear methods, data sources, maps, assumptions and recommendations—not only visual boards.
Start with assignments that fit the founder’s evidenced competence; bring in domain specialists where a tender, statute, client brief or environmental-clearance process requires them.
Use written scopes, deliverables, review milestones, data-ownership terms and professional-liability allocation from the first assignment.

sections.heading: Capabilities to productise
paragraphs: The strongest early positioning is usually a defined planning problem rather than a generic claim to provide all consulting services. B.Plan-aligned work can be packaged around baseline studies and spatial diagnostics; GIS and map production; land-use, site and neighbourhood planning; infrastructure and mobility analysis; regional and rural-development studies; environmental-planning inputs; stakeholder communication; and policy or project-appraisal notes.

Planning work should be evidence-led. The national B.Plan model curriculum explicitly combines planning theory and practice with quantitative and qualitative methods, spatial-data infrastructure, planning communication, land economics, development finance, legislation and project appraisal. This supports a consultancy model that connects mapped evidence, regulations, alternatives, implementation priorities and an auditable report rather than supplying drawings alone.
bullets: Spatial evidence: base maps, GIS databases, development profiles, survey design and analysis.
Plan and policy outputs: concept plans, land-use strategies, infrastructure frameworks, project notes and implementation roadmaps.
Environmental-planning support: ecology/resource and sustainability analysis within the firm’s competence; use accredited and multidisciplinary teams where formal EIA/EMP work is required.
Client-facing delivery: presentations, consultation materials, report writing, tender responses and document control.

sections.heading: A lean, multidisciplinary consulting model
paragraphs: A sensible launch model is a founder-led core team with a repeatable workflow: qualify the client problem; define scope, exclusions, data dependencies and acceptance criteria; price by named deliverables and review stages; perform field/data work; undertake internal quality review; issue draft and final reports; and retain a controlled project record. The curriculum emphasis on planning practice, communication, project formulation/appraisal/management and dissertation work supports this structured delivery approach.

The firm can scale through a vetted associate network—such as GIS analysts, transport planners, environmental scientists, economists, architects, legal advisers and survey specialists—rather than representing every discipline as in-house. This is particularly important where work requires regulated credentials or specialist evidence.

For public-sector discovery and procurement, GeM supports registration as a service provider. Its seller documentation identifies proprietorship, company, firm and trust/society as organisation configurations and describes service-provider registration; actual eligibility, category requirements and tender conditions remain buyer- and procurement-specific.
bullets: Choose a narrow initial offer, for example GIS-based development profiles, local-area planning support, policy research, or infrastructure diagnostics.
Maintain reusable templates for proposals, scopes, data registers, field-survey protocols, QA reviews, invoices and final report packs.
Avoid contingent commitments that depend on client-held data, statutory approvals or third-party surveys unless the scope allocates responsibility and time extensions clearly.
For government work, treat GeM registration as market access, not as qualification for a particular tender; verify each bid’s technical and financial criteria.

sections.heading: Registration, tax and regulated-work controls
paragraphs: Select the legal form with professional advice based on ownership, risk, contracting and future funding needs. If forming a company, MCA’s SPICe+ with AGILE-PRO-S is the government’s integrated incorporation route; its linked services include incorporation, PAN, TAN, DIN, GST registration where applied for, bank account and specified registrations. The precise filings and ongoing obligations depend on the selected entity and State.

Udyam registration can be considered for an eligible service enterprise. The Ministry of MSME portal says the process is online, paperless, free and based on self-declaration, and that an enterprise receives a permanent registration number with no renewal requirement. It is not a substitute for tax, labour, local, professional or sector-specific approvals.

For GST, section 22 of the CGST Act sets the general non-special-category threshold at aggregate turnover exceeding ₹20 lakh in a financial year. The rule has exceptions, including categories addressed by compulsory-registration provisions, and State/location and supply facts matter; confirm the current position with a qualified tax professional before deciding whether, when and where to register.

Do not market ordinary environmental-planning capability as authority to submit EIA/EMP documentation for environmental clearance. QCI states that MoEFCC has mandated consultants accredited under the QCI–NABET EIA scheme for preparation and presentation of EIA-EMPs to regulatory authorities. Before bidding on such work, verify the current accreditation scope, validity, sector/category coverage and client/regulator requirements.
bullets: Use engagement letters that state the exact service, standards, data supplied by the client, limitations, review process, payment milestones, confidentiality, intellectual-property rights and dispute terms.

Keep GST, income-tax, corporate/LLP or partnership, employment and local-establishment compliance on a calendar appropriate to the entity and State; engage a chartered accountant/company secretary/lawyer as applicable.
Conduct bid-by-bid compliance checks for public procurement, environmental approvals, professional credentials and requested insurance.
Maintain a conflict-of-interest register, source/data citation practice and version control for maps and reports.

sections.heading: Growth paths after establishing the practice
paragraphs: Growth can proceed by deepening a niche—such as GIS/spatial-data services, local-area and land-use planning, infrastructure and mobility studies, regional/rural planning, environmental planning or policy evaluation—or by becoming a multidisciplinary prime consultant with specialist partners. The B.Plan model curriculum provides a broad platform across these domains, while specialised planning programmes can support deeper technical capability.

A consulting firm that meets the notified conditions may consider DPIIT Startup recognition. Startup India states that eligible entities include private limited companies, registered partnership firms, LLPs and cooperative societies; it also requires an innovation/improvement or scalable-business proposition and applies turnover and age conditions. Recognition is not automatic and should be pursued only where the venture actually meets the current definition.

For public-sector sales, a firm can assess GeM service-provider registration and then respond selectively to opportunities matching its demonstrable credentials. As its track record grows, it can add stronger quality systems, sector specialists and, where relevant, the accreditation needed for regulated environmental-clearance assignments.
bullets: Early stage: founder-led assignments, a portfolio of completed studies and repeatable GIS/reporting processes.
Expansion stage: framework or retainer work, associate network, stronger tender capability and formal quality assurance.
Specialist stage: advanced planning degrees/training, sector expertise and any required accreditation before taking regulated work.
Institutional stage: multi-city delivery, public-procurement participation, documented methods and robust governance.
